name: manual-comparison-v1
description: A manual side-by-side comparison corpus of 20 fully synthetic writing tasks spanning five size tiers: tiny, short, medium, large, and very large.
sourceWordCountMethod: Whitespace-delimited words in the supplied source material only.

cases.title: Budget Update Copyedit
cases.prompt:
Copyedit the source for grammar, spelling, punctuation, and subject-verb agreement. Preserve every fact, number, and meaning. Do not add, remove, reorganize, or stylistically embellish content. Return one corrected paragraph only, with no title or commentary.

SOURCE
Yesterday, Mina send the revised budget to all three vendors, but two of them hasn't replied. The launch date remain August 14, and the total approved amount is $18,400.
END SOURCE
cases.outputConstraint: One corrected paragraph only; copyediting without adding, removing, reorganizing, or stylistically embellishing content; all facts, numbers, and meaning unchanged.

cases.title: The Shared Loaf
cases.prompt:
Rewrite the source as emotionally resonant microfiction. Preserve every stated event and object; do not invent names, backstory, dialogue, or a different ending. Use third person and 45-60 words. Return only the story, with no title or commentary.

SOURCE
At midnight, Jo found a warm loaf of bread on the empty subway seat. A paper tag read, "For the person who missed dinner." Jo took half, then left the rest at the next stop.
END SOURCE
cases.outputConstraint: A title-free, third-person story of 45-60 words that retains the setting, tag text, actions, and ending.

cases.title: Drift Mug Product Card
cases.prompt:
Write product card copy from the source. Preserve all facts exactly and do not invent materials, warranties, discounts, certifications, or performance claims. Format as: a headline of at most 7 words; one sentence of 18-28 words; exactly 3 bullet points; and a 2-4 word call to action. Keep the total under 75 words. Return only the copy.

SOURCE
Drift Mug is an insulated 12-ounce travel mug. It keeps drinks hot for six hours, has a leak-resistant sliding lid, fits standard cup holders, costs $28, and comes in navy or cream.
END SOURCE
cases.outputConstraint: Under 75 words, comprising one short headline, one 18-28-word sentence, exactly three bullets, and a 2-4-word CTA.

cases.title: Saturday Cardamom Buns
cases.prompt:
Turn the source into an Instagram caption that makes the announcement inviting without exaggerating urgency or inventing details. Preserve every fact. Begin with a hook, use at most one emoji, include a clear preorder call to action, and use no hashtags. Write 45-65 words in no more than 2 paragraphs. Return only the caption.

SOURCE
Nia's neighborhood bakery will offer cardamom buns this Saturday from 8 a.m. until sold out. Each bun costs $4. Preorders close Friday at noon, and pickup is at 17 Willow Street.
END SOURCE
cases.outputConstraint: A 45-65-word caption in at most two paragraphs, with a hook, no hashtags, at most one emoji, and no invented preorder method.

cases.title: Incorrect Tracking Link
cases.prompt:
Write a customer support email based only on the source. Preserve every date, time, status, and required action. Sound calm, accountable, and specific; do not blame the carrier or promise compensation. Format as a subject line followed by a body of 90-125 words in 2-3 short paragraphs. Return only the email.

SOURCE
On June 3, customers who ordered the Alder desk lamp received a shipping notice with an incorrect tracking link. The lamps are still scheduled to arrive between June 8 and June 10. The warehouse corrected the links on June 4. Customers do not need to place another order or contact the carrier. A replacement tracking email will arrive within 24 hours. Anyone who does not receive it by June 5 at 5 p.m. Eastern should reply to the original order confirmation.
END SOURCE
cases.outputConstraint: A subject line plus a 90-125-word body in two or three paragraphs, retaining every date, time, delivery status, and customer instruction.

cases.title: LoopBin Demonstration
cases.prompt:
Write a 45-second vertical video script based only on the source. Preserve every fact and do not imply the device creates soil. Use exactly 5 numbered shots. Each shot must have a VISUAL line and a VO line. Open with a curiosity hook and end with price and availability. Keep the total script 100-135 words. Return only the script.

SOURCE
Ravi is demonstrating a compact countertop composter called LoopBin. He adds coffee grounds, vegetable peels, and a paper filter, then locks the lid and taps the start button. The device runs quietly, but it does not produce finished soil; it dries and grinds food scraps to reduce their volume before they go into municipal compost collection. One cycle takes four hours. The video ends with Ravi emptying the processed material into a green curbside bin. LoopBin costs $149 and is available only in the United States.
END SOURCE
cases.outputConstraint: A 100-135-word, five-shot script with VISUAL and VO for every shot, a curiosity hook, accurate composting language, and price/availability at the end.

cases.title: Northstar Sync Rollout Incident
cases.prompt:
Turn the source into a public status-page update. Preserve every metric, timestamp, version, impact detail, remediation step, and user instruction. Do not speculate about the cause beyond the source or claim full resolution. Format as a 5-8-word title followed by 2 short paragraphs totaling 100-135 words. Lead with the current status. Return only the update.

SOURCE
Version 2.4 of Northstar Sync was deployed to 20% of workspaces at 09:00 UTC on Tuesday. At 09:37, monitoring showed a rise in duplicate calendar events for accounts using two-way Google Calendar sync. The rollout was paused at 09:42. Engineers traced the issue to retry logic introduced in version 2.4 and disabled that code path at 10:18. Duplicate creation stopped, and no events were deleted. Existing duplicates will be removed automatically by 18:00 UTC. The rollout remains paused while the team validates the fix. Users do not need to take action.
END SOURCE
cases.outputConstraint: A 5-8-word title and two paragraphs totaling 100-135 words, accurately retaining every metric and timestamp without overstating resolution.

cases.title: Riverlight Winter Meal
cases.prompt:
Write an event announcement for a website based only on the source. Preserve every logistical detail, eligibility rule, accessibility note, and contact detail. Do not imply that registration, payment, or donations are required. Format as a headline, a 2-sentence inviting introduction, then exactly 5 labeled lines: WHEN, WHERE, ACCESS, VOLUNTEER, CONTACT. Keep the total between 120 and 165 words. Return only the announcement.

SOURCE
Riverlight Community Kitchen is holding a free winter meal on July 27 at Harbor Hall, 220 East Pine Avenue. Doors open at 5:30 p.m., and dinner is served from 6 to 8 p.m. The event is open to everyone; no registration or proof of income is required. Vegetarian meals and wheelchair-accessible seating will be available. Children must attend with an adult. The organizers are seeking volunteers for setup from 3 to 5 p.m. and cleanup from 8 to 9 p.m. Volunteers must be at least 16. Donations are welcome but not required. Questions can be sent to hello@riverlight.example.
END SOURCE
cases.outputConstraint: A 120-165-word announcement with the specified headline, introduction, and five labeled lines, retaining every participation, accessibility, volunteer, and donation condition.

cases.title: Why One City Block Stays Hotter Than Another
cases.prompt:
Rewrite the source below as a polished educational explainer for curious general readers.

Requirements:
- Use 240–310 words.
- Include a clear title and 2–4 short paragraphs.
- Preserve every factual claim, causal qualification, tradeoff, and distinction in the source.
- Keep the distinction between surface temperature, air temperature, and personal heat exposure clear.
- Introduce “evapotranspiration” in language a general reader can understand.
- Use a calm, precise voice. Do not use hype or alarmist language.
- Do not add facts, examples, statistics, recommendations, or citations.
- Return only the rewritten explainer.

Source:

On a hot afternoon, two blocks in the same city can have different temperatures. This is part of the urban heat island effect. Roads, roofs, and walls absorb solar energy during the day. Many of these surfaces release stored heat after sunset, so some neighborhoods cool slowly at night. Traffic, industry, and air conditioners also release waste heat, although their contribution varies by place and time.

Trees can reduce heat in two ways. Their leaves block sunlight before it reaches pavement and buildings. They also move water from soil to air through evapotranspiration, which uses energy and can cool the surrounding area. That second benefit depends on tree health and available water. A young tree in dry, compacted soil will not perform like a mature tree with enough root space.

Temperature maps require care. A satellite usually measures surface temperature: how hot a roof, road, or patch of grass appears from above. A weather station measures air temperature at a particular height and location. Neither measurement alone describes a person’s full heat exposure, which also depends on shade, humidity, wind, activity, and time of day.

The hottest areas often have less tree canopy, but heat risk is not only an engineering problem. Past investment decisions can shape where pavement, housing, parks, and shade are located. Reflective roofs, trees, shade structures, and lighter pavement can all help, but each works on a different timescale and has tradeoffs. A useful heat plan therefore begins with local measurements and the people most exposed, not one citywide average.
cases.outputConstraint: 240–310 words; title and 2–4 short paragraphs; preserve every claim, qualification, tradeoff, and measurement distinction; explain evapotranspiration in context; add no facts, examples, statistics, recommendations, or citations; return only the explainer.

cases.title: The Reminder Problem That Wasn't About Forgetting
cases.prompt:
Rewrite the source below as a concise case study for leaders of libraries, cooperatives, and other community services.

Requirements:
- Use 260–330 words.
- Include a title and a readable narrative body.
- Preserve every number, cost, operational detail, limitation, and next step.
- Make the original problem, discovery, pilot, result, and decision easy to follow.
- Preserve the distinction between shifting staff work and eliminating it.
- Do not imply that the pilot proved causation.
- Do not invent quotations, testimonials, motives, or benefits.
- Use a credible, restrained professional voice rather than promotional copy.
- Return only the rewritten case study.

Source:

North Ferry Tool Library lends drills, saws, sanders, and garden equipment to 1,900 members. At the start of 2025, 31 percent of Saturday reservations ended as no-shows. Reserved tools stayed off the shelf for 48 hours, so another member could see an item marked unavailable even when it was sitting behind the desk. Two staff members also spent the first hour of each Saturday calling people who had forgotten their pickup.

The team first proposed adding more reminder emails. Before doing that, they interviewed 12 members. Seven said the main problem was not forgetting. They did not know when a returned tool was actually ready, because the booking confirmation showed the reservation date but not the item’s check-in status.

The library ran an eight-Saturday pilot covering 246 reservations. Members received a message when the item was checked in, selected either a 10 a.m.–noon or noon–2 p.m. pickup window, and received one reminder two hours before that window. The setup cost $480, and the messaging service cost $18 per month.

During the pilot, the Saturday no-show rate fell from 31 percent to 19 percent. Median desk wait fell from 11 minutes to 6 minutes. Staff stopped making routine reminder calls, although 14 members requested phone support and continued receiving it. The change shifted staff time rather than eliminating it: someone still had to mark each returned item as ready.

The library kept the workflow for Saturdays. It did not claim that messaging alone caused the improvement. The pilot took place during the quieter winter season, covered only one location, and had no randomized comparison group. The next step is to test weekday reservations and measure how often ready-status messages arrive late.
cases.outputConstraint: 260–330 words; title and narrative body; preserve all figures, costs, operational details, limitations, ongoing work, phone support, and next step; do not imply causation or invent quotations, testimonials, motives, or benefits; return only the case study.

cases.title: The Week Our Spreadsheet Sold 249 Imaginary Mugs
cases.prompt:
Rewrite the source below as a founder newsletter for independent makers and small-shop owners.

Requirements:
- Use 300–380 words.
- Provide one subject line followed by the newsletter body.
- Preserve the first-person perspective and the dry, self-aware humor.
- Preserve every number, time, customer option, outcome, operational change, and consequence.
- Keep the events in their correct sequence.
- Retain the invitation for readers to reply.
- Do not invent dialogue, customer reactions, urgency, or business results.
- Do not turn it into promotional copy or a generic list of lessons.
- Return only the finished newsletter.

Source:

Subject: The week our spreadsheet sold 249 imaginary mugs

On Tuesday at 8:12 a.m., I reopened the shop with 642 blue mugs showing in inventory. At 8:35, after 417 orders had arrived, Lina walked into the packing room and asked where I planned to find the other mugs.

The spreadsheet had counted every formed mug as finished stock. Of the 642, 390 were still waiting for their first firing and 84 had failed the glaze check. We had 168 blue mugs ready to ship. This is a poor number to learn after accepting 417 orders.

I closed the shop and emailed everyone before noon. Customers could keep the blue mug and wait, switch to the sand color, or take a full refund. In the end, 296 waited, 83 switched, and 38 chose refunds. We rented two evening kiln slots from the community college. Every retained order shipped within 12 days, which was two days later than the estimate in my email. We refunded shipping on 27 orders that missed birthday dates customers had mentioned.

The problem was not really that the spreadsheet made a mistake. I had asked one cell to represent four different states: formed, fired, inspected, and boxed. It did exactly what I told it to do, with the confidence of a tiny accountant who has never seen a kiln.

We now scan each batch at all four stages. The website only counts inspected mugs, and Lina receives an alert if the physical shelf count differs from the system by more than six. The new process takes about nine extra minutes per batch. That is cheaper than borrowing a college kiln at night and composing 417 apologies.

If you run a small shop, hit reply and tell me which spreadsheet cell currently knows too much. I promise not to judge it until I have judged mine again.
cases.outputConstraint: 300–380 words; one subject line and newsletter body; preserve first person, dry humor, all figures, sequence, options, outcomes, operational changes, and reply invitation; invent no dialogue, reactions, urgency, or results; return only the newsletter.

cases.title: Making the Library Noisy Again
cases.prompt:
Rewrite the source below as approximately three minutes of spoken remarks for the reopening of a neighborhood library.

Requirements:
- Use 330–410 words.
- Make it sound natural when delivered aloud.
- Keep the voice warm, grounded, grateful, and gently funny without becoming sentimental.
- Preserve every name, number, time, comparison, funding amount, operational change, and acknowledgment.
- Preserve the qualification that temporary-site visits were lower than the prior year even though they exceeded expectations.
- Do not add anecdotes, quotations, claims, or organizations.
- Keep the final invitation and its meaning as the ending.
- Return only the finished speech.

Source:

Good evening, and welcome back to the East Branch Library. Six months ago, at 2:13 on a Monday morning, a pipe split above the children’s room. Water reached three shelves before the night custodian, Mr. Alvarez, found it. Staff and volunteers moved 8,400 books before breakfast. We still lost 1,126 books, the carpet, and most of the painted paper moon that used to hang over the reading rug.

The building closed, but the library did not stop. The fire station lent us its meeting room for Tuesday story time. The high school put two rolling shelves in its lobby. Farah drove the bookmobile to the east market on Thursdays, including the Thursday when the heater failed and everyone kept their coats on inside. I am grateful to each of those partners.

We expected people to wait for the branch to reopen. Instead, they followed it around. Over six months, the temporary sites recorded 19,044 visits. That is 11 percent fewer visits than this building recorded in the same months last year, but it is far more than we predicted for a library without a library. Two hundred and seven residents also registered for their first card.

The repairs cost $286,000. Insurance covered $214,000, the city covered $52,000, and 613 donors supplied the remaining $20,000. The new carpet is less celestial than the old one. It is, however, waterproof underneath, which is a quality I have recently learned to admire.

Tonight we are reopening the same address with a few changes. The children’s shelves are on raised bases. A moisture alarm now calls three people instead of leaving one custodian to discover a flood. The paper moon could not be saved, so students from East High made a new one from pages of books that were already beyond repair.

Please thank the staff, the volunteers, our temporary hosts, the repair crews, and Mr. Alvarez. Then please do the thing that made all of this work worthwhile: borrow something, sit down, and make the building noisy again.
cases.outputConstraint: 330–410 words; approximately three minutes when spoken; preserve all names, figures, comparisons, funding, changes, acknowledgments, and the temporary-site qualification; add no anecdotes, quotations, claims, or organizations; retain the final invitation as the ending; return only the speech.

cases.title: The Warning From 2:24
cases.prompt:
Rewrite the source below as a polished speculative-fiction scene.

Requirements:
- Use 380–470 words.
- Write in past tense and close third-person limited to Nora.
- Use a spare, precise, quietly unsettling voice.
- Preserve every time, event, clue, action, line of quoted dialogue, and piece of technical behavior.
- Preserve the sequence of events and the causal logic of the ending.
- Do not add backstory, characters, technology, scientific explanations, or a resolution beyond the source.
- Do not make Nora know anything she does not observe or infer in the source.
- Return only the rewritten scene.

Source:

At 2:16 a.m., Nora heard her own voice come through the observatory speaker.

Dish Seven was offline with the rest of the western array, and the maintenance channel was not recording. Even so, the voice said, “Do not restart Seven.” Three quick taps followed. Nora always tapped the desk that way before a difficult calibration. She took her hand away from the console.

The restart was scheduled for 2:20. Malik was already outside replacing a relay at the base of the dish. He had told her to run the standard sequence when the green indicator returned. The indicator turned green.

Nora checked the channel history. It showed no incoming transmission. She replayed the buffer. Her warning was there, thin under the static, followed by the three taps. The time stamp read 2:24 a.m., eight minutes in the future.

She called Malik. Wind scraped across his microphone. He said the relay was seated and asked why the motors had not started. Nora told him about the warning. There was a pause. Then he laughed once, not because it was funny but because he did that when a problem lacked a form.

He asked whether she had slept. She said yes. He asked whether lightning could have crossed the maintenance band. The nearest storm was forty kilometers east. He reminded her that the array had produced stray echoes during last month’s software update. She reminded him that none of those echoes had used her voice.

At 2:19, Malik told her to restart at low speed while he watched from the service trench. The procedure allowed it. Nora put her thumb on the control.

The three taps sounded again.

This time Malik heard them too. He stopped speaking. Nora canceled the sequence and told him to leave the trench. He had taken six steps when a support bolt snapped. The upper assembly dropped half a meter and jammed against the safety collar. If the dish had begun turning, the service ladder would have swept through the trench.

At 2:24, the maintenance channel opened by itself. Nora’s microphone light came on. On the console, a blank recording began to fill from right to left.

Malik whispered her name through the speaker.

Nora leaned toward the microphone. She knew what she had to say, because she had already heard herself say it.
cases.outputConstraint: 380–470 words; past tense and close third-person limited to Nora; preserve all timing, events, clues, actions, quoted dialogue, technical behavior, sequence, and ending logic; add no backstory, characters, technology, explanation, later resolution, or knowledge beyond Nora; return only the scene.

cases.title: Decision on Automated Meeting Summary Expansion
cases.prompt:
Rewrite the source below as a decision-ready executive memo for a product council.

Requirements:
- Use 360–450 words.
- Retain the To, From, and Subject lines.
- Put the requested decision first, then organize the evidence, risks, proposed extension, and decision criteria with concise headings.
- Preserve every number, percentage, threshold, deadline, cost, caveat, attribution limit, recommendation, and proposed action.
- Keep measured results distinct from survey responses and commercial signals.
- Do not imply that the feature caused productivity gains or renewals.
- Do not soften or bury the accuracy, retention, and cost risks.
- Do not add analysis, evidence, options, or recommendations.
- Use a direct, formal, non-dramatic voice.
- Return only the rewritten memo.

Source:

To: Product Council
From: Amina Shah, VP Product
Subject: Decision on expansion of automated meeting summaries

Decision requested: approve a six-week extension of the pilot for the existing 18 enterprise accounts, but do not expand access and do not keep summaries enabled by default.

The four-week pilot covered 4,860 meetings. Sixty-two percent of eligible users enabled a summary at least once, and 41 percent used the feature in three or more weeks. Among 73 survey respondents, the median reported time spent preparing follow-up notes fell from 12 minutes to 7 minutes. The survey response rate was 28 percent, and we did not instrument preparation time, so this result is directional rather than a measured productivity gain.

The risk indicators are above the launch threshold. Support received 37 factual-correction tickets, compared with our projection of 9. Six tickets involved an incorrect customer name or commitment, although none of those summaries were sent outside the account before correction. Three accounts disabled the feature because their legal teams wanted clearer retention controls. Inference cost averaged $0.14 per meeting, 40 percent above the target.

There are positive commercial signals, but they should not be treated as attribution. Two account teams said the summaries were mentioned favorably during renewal conversations. Both renewals also included pricing changes and service commitments. Sales has not claimed that the feature caused either renewal.

Engineering estimates that citation links to the source transcript will take three weeks. An account-level retention control will take five weeks. Turning the feature off by default can be completed in two days. The proposed extension would cost approximately $18,000 in engineering time and $680 in inference charges.

During the extension, we would turn summaries off by default, add citation links, test the retention control with the three accounts that raised concerns, and log preparation time for consenting users. Expansion would return to the council only if weekly repeat use reaches 50 percent, severe correction tickets remain at or below 0.2 percent of meetings, and average inference cost falls to $0.11 per meeting. If those conditions are not met, the team will present a narrower use case or a shutdown plan.

This recommendation preserves the opportunity to test demonstrated interest while limiting exposure to accuracy, retention, and cost risks. Approval is needed by Friday to avoid releasing the current engineering team to other commitments.
cases.outputConstraint: 360–450 words; retain To, From, and Subject; decision first with concise headings; preserve all figures, thresholds, deadlines, costs, caveats, attribution limits, recommendations, and actions; distinguish measured, surveyed, and commercial signals; do not imply causation, bury risks, or add analysis; return only the memo.

cases.title: The Repair Library
cases.prompt:
Rewrite the draft below as a polished long-form magazine article for a curious general audience. Make it absorbing while staying faithful to the source. Preserve every concrete fact, number, date, and name. Keep all quoted wording verbatim. Do not introduce outside information, invented scenes, invented dialogue, or claims not supported by the draft. You may reorder material and paraphrase unquoted prose. Write 700-850 words. Include a strong title and no subheadings. Output only the finished article.

DRAFT

On the first Saturday of every month, people line up outside a former bus depot on Maple Street carrying objects that do not work. They bring lamps with frayed cords, kettles that no longer heat, bicycles with slipping chains, and chairs with loose legs. The building is now the Harbor Junction Repair Library. It lends tools during the week and holds a repair clinic on Saturday.

The library opened in April 2023. A winter storm had flooded the depot the year before, and the city planned to leave it empty. Ana Ribeiro, who had organized neighborhood cleanups, proposed using it as a place where residents could borrow equipment instead of buying it. The city agreed to a two-year trial and charged one dollar per year in rent. Volunteers spent eleven weekends clearing mud, painting walls, and building shelves from damaged shipping pallets.

The first inventory had 214 items. It now has 1,386, including drills, sewing machines, pruning saws, tile cutters, cake pans, and a thermal camera. Members pay twenty-four dollars a year, though the fee is waived for anyone who asks. There were 1,842 active members at the end of 2025. The library recorded 9,104 loans that year. The most borrowed object was a pressure washer, checked out 173 times.

Ribeiro says the numbers are useful, but they miss what happens around the objects. “A drill may be gone for three hours, but the conversation about the shelf can last all afternoon,” she said. She has watched strangers exchange phone numbers after discovering that they live on the same block. She has also seen people return equipment early because another member needed it.

The repair clinic began when Mateo Singh, a retired elevator technician, offered to sit at a folding table with a multimeter. Six people came to the first clinic. Now the library limits appointments to forty and reserves ten walk-in slots. In 2025, volunteers examined 612 objects and returned 438 of them to working order. They do not repair microwaves, gas appliances, or anything whose safety they cannot verify.

Not every object is saved. On a rainy Saturday in November, Lila Chen brought in a blue table fan that had belonged to her grandmother. Singh found that the motor windings had burned. A replacement would cost more than a new fan. Chen decided not to repair it, but she removed the metal badge from its center before placing the fan in the recycling bin. “I thought success meant taking it home,” she said. “It turned out success was knowing why I couldn’t.”

The library has had problems. Three cordless drills disappeared during its first summer. A donated ladder failed inspection. Demand for weekend loans is higher than the supply of popular tools. The city’s trial ended in March 2025, and the council approved only eighteen months of additional funding, worth $96,000. To cover the rest of its budget, the library sells memberships, runs classes, and receives donations from two local hardware stores.

A university team surveyed 327 members in October 2025. Seventy-one percent said they had avoided at least one purchase because of the library, and 46 percent said they had attempted a repair they otherwise would not have tried. The survey relied on self-reported answers and did not calculate money saved or waste prevented.

At closing time, the staff checks every returned case. Bits go back into labeled drawers. Batteries are connected to chargers. Dust is brushed from saw guards. Ribeiro often finds handwritten notes inside the cases. One said that the borrowed sander had helped finish a crib before a baby arrived. Another simply said, “Worked. Neighbor wants it next.” Ribeiro keeps the notes in a jar behind the desk. She says the jar is the one thing in the building that cannot be borrowed.
cases.outputConstraint: 700-850 words; include one title and no subheadings; output only the finished article.

cases.title: One Winter Run of the Kestrel
cases.prompt:
Turn the production notes below into a polished documentary-style YouTube script for a curious general audience. Preserve the chronology of the trip and every concrete fact, number, date, name, and qualification. Keep all quoted wording verbatim. Do not add outside information, invented dialogue, unsupported visuals, or sounds that the notes do not support. Write 850-1000 words. Include a title line and format the body using [VISUAL], NARRATOR:, ON-SCREEN TEXT:, and named on-camera speaker labels where useful. Do not add host banter, sponsor copy, or a call to action. Output only the finished script.

PRODUCTION NOTES

The video follows one winter supply run of the ferry Kestrel from Port Alder to Gull Island. The island is fourteen kilometers from the mainland and has 312 year-round residents. From November through March, the Kestrel makes one round trip on Monday, Wednesday, and Friday. There is no bridge and no scheduled air service.

Filming took place on January 18, 2026. The shift began at 1:40 a.m. at a warehouse beside the Port Alder terminal. Snow was falling but not accumulating. Deckhand June Okafor checked each item against a paper manifest and marked it with one of three colored tags: green for the general store, blue for the clinic, and orange for a household pickup. The load included twenty-three grocery crates, twelve propane cylinders, two boxes of library books, a replacement pump for the school boiler, and a locked medical cooler containing insulin. The cooler had a temperature display on its lid. It read 4.1 degrees Celsius when it was loaded.

Captain Elsie Ward arrived at 2:05 a.m. She has worked the route for seventeen years and has captained the Kestrel since 2020. Before departure, she reviewed wind, visibility, and wave readings, then walked the deck with Okafor. Engineer Ivo Marin started the main engine and tested the bow thruster. Ward said, “Most nights, the job is making ordinary things arrive ordinarily.”

The Kestrel is twenty-eight meters long and can carry forty-eight passengers, although only three traveled on this run. It entered service in 2019, replacing a larger car ferry that required nine crew members. The Kestrel carries no private cars. Its battery system powers hotel loads and allows it to leave and enter the two harbors without running the main engine. It does not operate on batteries for the full crossing. According to the port authority’s maintenance summary, fuel use on the route fell 22 percent during the Kestrel’s first full year, but the summary did not separate the effect of the new vessel from changes in the timetable.

Loading ended at 2:47 a.m. Okafor secured the propane cylinders in an open rack and placed the medical cooler in the heated cabin. The ferry left at 3:02. For the first six minutes, the loudest sounds on deck were the water against the hull and the electric bow thruster. The main engine started after the ferry passed the harbor marker.

Halfway across, visibility dropped from five kilometers to roughly one. Ward reduced speed from twelve knots to nine. The radar showed a small target crossing from the north. It was later visible as an unlit fishing skiff. Ward altered course by seven degrees and sounded one long blast. The skiff passed behind the ferry at 3:31. Nobody on either vessel spoke by radio. Ward logged the encounter and restored course. “You don’t get points for being surprised correctly,” she said afterward. “You make room before surprise becomes the problem.”

In the engine room, Marin inspected a thin line of condensation under a ventilation duct. He wiped it, checked the tray beneath it, and took a photograph for the maintenance log. He said winter creates small problems that become large if they remain wet. Marin was raised on Gull Island and moved to the mainland at twenty. His mother still lives three streets uphill from the island terminal. He does not visit her during the stop because unloading time is short.

The Kestrel arrived at Gull Island at 3:58. Five people were waiting under the terminal lights. Store owner Darius Bell moved the grocery crates onto a trailer. Clinic nurse Nora Vale checked the cooler display, which read 4.3 degrees, and signed the manifest. School custodian Tom Arlen collected the boiler pump. The school’s old pump was still working but had begun making a grinding noise two days earlier. Arlen planned to replace it before classes started.

Unloading took nineteen minutes. The return cargo included empty crates, outgoing mail, a cooler of water samples from the clinic, and a cello in a hard case belonging to sixteen-year-old Mara Bell. She was traveling to a regional audition and was one of four passengers on the return trip. She sat with the case between her knees rather than placing it in the luggage rack. She said, “If the ferry doesn’t go, neither do I.”

The vessel departed the island at 4:21. Dawn had not begun when it reached Port Alder at 5:17. Okafor unloaded the water samples first. By 5:36, the deck was empty except for melted snow around the tie-down points. Ward completed the log, Marin shut down the main engine, and Okafor placed the three rolls of colored tags back in a locker. The next manifest was already clipped to the warehouse wall.
cases.outputConstraint: 850-1000 words; include a title line and specified script labels; output only the finished script.

cases.title: Locker 214
cases.prompt:
Rewrite the draft below as a publishable scene from a literary suspense novel. Keep third-person close point of view on Mira and keep past tense. Preserve the sequence of plot events, all named places and times, the established relationships and backstory, every discovered piece of evidence, and the ending situation. Do not add characters, discoveries, memories, explanations, or plot events. You may rewrite dialogue for flow, but do not change its factual meaning. Write 800-950 words. Do not include a title or section heading. Output only the finished scene.

DRAFT

Mira reached the old Bellweather Station at 11:46 p.m., fourteen minutes before Elias had said he would lock the side door. Rain covered the clock face and ran down the plywood over the ticket windows. The station had been closed for six years. Demolition was scheduled to begin at seven in the morning.

She entered through the baggage corridor. Her flashlight showed peeling green paint, a row of hooks, and chalk numbers that no longer matched any trains. The building smelled damp. Somewhere under the floor, water made a regular dripping sound. She told herself that she only needed to reach the lost-property office, open locker 214, and leave.

Her father had given her the locker key three days before he died. He had been a brake inspector for Bellweather Rail. For eleven years, Mira had believed the official finding that he had missed a cracked coupling before the South Cut derailment. Four people were injured in that accident. On his last afternoon in the hospital, he told her that inspection reports had been changed and that the original ledger was in locker 214. He was too weak to explain who had changed them.

Elias waited beside the stairs to platform four. He wore his security uniform, though the station no longer had regular security. A ring of keys hung from his belt. He and Mira had grown up on the same street, but they had not spoken since her father’s funeral.

“You’re late,” he said.

“I’m early.”

“For changing your mind, you’re late.”

He explained that a demolition supervisor was doing one final walkthrough at midnight. They had perhaps ten minutes. Mira asked why he had agreed to help. Elias said the company had stopped paying for overnight guards two months earlier, but his access card still worked until the building was transferred to the contractor. That did not answer her question, and they both knew it.

They went down the stairs. The concourse lights were off. When lightning flashed through the high windows, the departure board appeared for a moment with all its blank rows. Elias unlocked a steel gate, and they crossed the waiting hall. Mira remembered buying a paper cup of cocoa there with her father when she was eight. She did not mention it.

The lost-property office was behind the former information desk. Locker 214 stood in the bottom row. Mira pushed in her father’s brass key. It entered halfway and stopped. She tried again. Elias crouched beside her and said the locker cores had been replaced after a break-in in 2022. Mira accused him of knowing that before she came. He said he had hoped her father’s key would still work.

A door slammed somewhere above them. Elias looked toward the ceiling and told her the supervisor had arrived. He took a flat emergency tool from his belt and inserted it beside the locker latch. Mira asked whether opening it would trigger an alarm. Elias said, “Only one way to make that a useful question.”

He forced the latch. A bell began ringing in the corridor, not loud but steady. Inside the locker was a black ledger wrapped in a grocery bag. Mira opened it on the floor. The first pages listed inspection dates, car numbers, and initials in her father’s handwriting. Several entries had red circles beside them. The final twelve pages had been cut out close to the binding.

Mira said the missing pages were the only ones that mattered. Elias told her to take the ledger anyway. Footsteps crossed the waiting hall. A beam of light moved under the office door.

Mira noticed a gray paper pad beneath the ledger. It was the sort of blotter her father kept on his desk. The top sheet looked empty, but when she tilted it toward her flashlight, she saw shallow lines pressed into the paper. She rubbed the side of a pencil across one corner. A reversed car number appeared, followed by part of a date. The removed ledger pages had been written while resting on the pad.

Elias switched off her flashlight. The supervisor tried the office handle. Elias placed one hand over the ringing alarm bell to muffle it. In the dark, he whispered that a service hatch behind the shelving opened into the parcel tunnel. Mira put the ledger under her coat and folded the blotter once. Her father had always told her never to fold evidence, but the pad would not fit otherwise.

The office door shook as the supervisor pushed against it. Elias moved the lowest shelf, revealing a square opening. Mira crawled through first. The parcel tunnel was narrow, and rainwater covered its floor. Elias followed and pulled the shelf back into place. They moved without lights while the alarm continued behind them.

At the far end, Elias opened a hatch onto platform four. Rain blew under the canopy. Beyond the fence, the first demolition excavator turned into the station yard six hours early, its amber beacon rotating across the tracks. Mira held the folded blotter inside her coat and watched the light find them.
cases.outputConstraint: 800-950 words; third-person close on Mira and past tense; no title or heading; output only the scene.

cases.title: The Door, the Clock, and the Proof
cases.prompt:
Rewrite the draft below as a polished keynote section meant to be spoken aloud to a general professional audience. Keep the first-person perspective. Preserve every concrete fact, number, sequence, example, and causal qualification. Keep quoted wording verbatim. Do not introduce outside claims, invented dialogue, or invented details. Write 1000-1200 words. Do not include a title, headings, bullet points, or stage directions. Output only the finished keynote section.

DRAFT

I used to believe that when a useful program failed, the problem was motivation. If people did not show up, complete the form, or finish the course, I assumed they did not care enough. That belief made me efficient at blaming the person who was absent.

I learned otherwise at twenty-six, when I coordinated grocery pickups at the Cedar Street Community Kitchen. We had registered 180 households for a new weekly food box. We had enough volunteers, enough boxes, and a spreadsheet with every name. On the first Thursday, only sixty-four households collected their food. We stood beside 116 boxes at closing time and tried to explain the gap.

Our explanations were flattering to us. People had forgotten. People were disorganized. People had signed up without being serious. The next week, we sent three reminder texts instead of one. Sixty-eight households came. We had improved by four and congratulated ourselves for using data.

One of the people who did not come was Alina Torres. I called her and asked why. She said she had tried. I heard that sentence as an excuse, so she invited me to try with her the following Thursday.

Alina finished cleaning offices at 4:30 p.m. The kitchen distributed boxes from four to six. The bus that stopped near her building was scheduled for 5:12, and the next one came forty minutes later. We got off at 5:34 and walked around the kitchen because the entrance on Cedar Street was locked. A paper sign said pickups were through the courtyard, but rain had folded the bottom half of the sign over the arrow. We found the courtyard at 5:43.

At the table, a volunteer asked Alina for identification. Identification was not required; we already had a check-in list. The volunteer had begun asking because two boxes had gone to the wrong people the week before. Alina’s wallet was in a locker at work. I was standing beside her, wearing the kitchen’s blue staff shirt, and the volunteer still hesitated. We got the box at 5:51. Then we carried it thirteen blocks because Alina had missed the return bus.

She had remembered. She was organized. She was serious. Our program had converted a box of groceries into a forty-one-minute bus wait, a hidden entrance, an unofficial document check, and a long walk with twelve kilograms of food. Then we had described the leftover boxes as a failure of motivation.

Over the next two weeks, we changed four things. Pickup stayed open until 7:30. We put a weatherproof sign at both entrances. We stopped asking for identification. We added a cart that volunteers could take to the bus stop. On the first Thursday after those changes, 151 households collected their boxes. We had not delivered a speech about responsibility. We had moved the obstacles.

That experience gave me a question I now use whenever I hear, “They just won’t do it.” My question is: what does doing it actually require?

I organize the answer into three parts: the door, the clock, and the proof. The door is how someone finds and enters the experience. The clock is whether the experience fits into the time they really have, not the time we imagine they have. The proof is what they must know, possess, or reveal before the system agrees to help them.

Years later, my team used this test on an employee training portal. Sixty-two percent of invited staff started a required safety course, but only 19 percent finished it by the deadline. The first theory was that employees found safety training boring. That may have been true, but it was not the first useful fact.

To begin the course, an employee followed a link from email, signed into a separate learning account, confirmed a code, selected a regional policy, entered a manager number, and opened the first module. We counted nine clicks before any teaching began. The session expired after fifteen minutes of inactivity, and progress within a module was not saved. Night-shift workers often took the course at a shared terminal while also answering radio calls. The system treated each interruption as abandonment.

We reduced the path to three clicks, used the existing company login, saved progress after every screen, and replaced the manager number with a prefilled field. Completion by the deadline rose to 51 percent in the next course cycle. This was not a controlled experiment; the course content and supervisor reminders also changed. I cannot claim that fewer clicks caused the entire increase. I can say that employees stopped calling the help desk to ask where to find a manager number, because they no longer needed one.

Removing friction is not the same as making everything effortless. Some effort is the point. A piano student has to practice scales. A pilot has to complete a checklist. A voter may need to consider competing claims. The goal is not to erase meaningful work. It is to separate the effort that creates value from the effort that merely guards the doorway.

A public library once asked me to look at late returns after it removed overdue fines. The staff expected returns to improve, but they did not. We examined the reminder emails and the account page. Both were clear. Then we visited the after-hours return slot. The slot had a metal turn that jammed when more than seven books collected behind it. On busy evenings, patrons pushed a book halfway in, felt resistance, and took it home again. The policy had changed. The door had not.

There is also emotional friction. A form can be short and still make someone feel accused. A help desk can be open and still require a person to announce a private problem within earshot of strangers. A button labeled “failure review” can lead to a useful process that nobody wants to start. Time and clicks are easy to count; uncertainty and shame are easier to ignore.

That is why a friction audit cannot happen entirely in a meeting room. Walk the route. Use the old phone. Arrive after the bus. Read the form without the vocabulary your team shares. Ask someone where they expected the door to be. Do not begin by asking why people fail your system. Begin by discovering the moment your system stops deserving their effort.

I keep a photograph from Cedar Street. It shows rows of white grocery boxes under fluorescent lights after that first pickup. I used to see waste in that picture. Now I see 116 journeys we had never bothered to map. Every unopened box was evidence that our good intention had ended too early.
cases.outputConstraint: 1000-1200 words; spoken first-person prose with no title, headings, bullets, or stage directions; output only the finished section.

cases.title: 814 Kilometers
cases.prompt:
Rewrite the draft below as a publishable personal essay for a literary magazine. Keep the first-person reflective perspective. Preserve every event, timeline detail, name, number, material fact, and quoted statement. Keep the uncertainty about the Northbridge trip unresolved. Do not invent scenes, dialogue, motives, memories, or conclusions. You may reorder material if the chronology remains clear. Write 1000-1200 words. Include a strong title and no subheadings. Output only the finished essay.

DRAFT

My father left me a car I had spent half my life making fun of. It was a teal 1998 Vela hatchback with one pale replacement door and 241,806 kilometers on the odometer. He died in late February at sixty-seven. By April, my sister and I had cleared most of his house, but the car remained under the carport, gathering yellow pollen in the seams around its windows.

My sister wanted to call a scrap yard. I said I would handle it because I lived nine hundred kilometers away and could leave without having to watch an empty space where the car had been. I planned to clean it on Saturday, sell it on Sunday, and take the train home on Monday.

My father had treated maintenance as a form of record keeping. In the glove compartment, I found a black notebook with a cracked elastic strap. Every fuel stop since 2004 had its own line: date, odometer, liters, price. He calculated consumption to one decimal place. Repairs were written in capital letters. WIPERS. BELT. LEFT BEARING. On the final page, three weeks before his death, he had written BATTERY WEAK and underlined it twice.

There were no descriptions of where he had gone or why. My father believed a trip began when the engine started and ended when the numbers balanced. As a child, I learned not to ask how much longer a drive would take. He would tap the fuel gauge and say, “The car knows.” I understood that he did not.

The Vela had taken me to swimming lessons, orthodontist appointments, two summer jobs, and the house of every friend whose parents were unwilling to drive me home. Its ceiling fabric sagged above the back seat. The heater produced a sweet smell. At highway speed, the passenger window whistled. My father could identify speed from the whistle within five kilometers per hour, a skill that seemed impressive until I realized he could have repaired the seal.

When I was nineteen, we had our largest argument inside that car. I had accepted a place in a writing program in Northbridge. He called the program an expensive delay before real work. I called his life a list of tasks he had completed for people he did not like. He pulled into a rest area beside the toll road. We sat there with the engine running. He said, “You can go wherever you want, but don’t make leaving a personality.” I got out and called a friend to collect me. For the next eleven years, neither of us mentioned the rest area.

On the Saturday after his funeral, I vacuumed the Vela. Under the driver’s seat were two peppermints fused to their wrappers, a parking token, and a small brass screw. The trunk contained jumper cables, a folded wool blanket, and four glass jars filled with different sizes of bolts. I threw away the peppermints and kept moving the other objects from one side of the driveway to the other because I could not decide which of them counted as useful.

A mechanic from town came that afternoon. He started the engine, listened for less than a minute, and offered four hundred dollars. He said the transmission might last a week or two years. I told him I would think about it. After he left, the battery failed exactly as the notebook had predicted.

My father’s neighbor, Mrs. Vale, saw me connecting the charger. She asked whether I had found a buyer. Her niece Imani was starting clinical placements at the regional hospital and needed a cheap car for the early shift. I warned her about the transmission. Mrs. Vale said warning people was free.

Imani arrived on Sunday morning wearing hospital scrubs under her coat. She walked around the Vela once and asked if the color was original. I said most of it was. We took a test drive. I showed her how to lift the gear lever slightly before shifting into reverse and how to strike the dashboard above the radio when the display disappeared. Repeating my father’s instructions in my own voice made them sound less like criticism.

At a traffic light, Imani opened the black notebook. A folded fuel receipt slipped from the back. The receipt was from Northbridge, dated September 18, six years earlier. I knew the date because it was the night of my first public reading. My father had told me he could not attend because two people were absent at work.

After the test drive, I took the notebook into the kitchen and went backward through the entries. On September 18, the odometer increased by 814 kilometers. There was fuel in Northbridge at 6:42 p.m. and another purchase near his home at 2:11 the next morning. My reading had started at seven. He could have been in the room. I remembered every face I had looked for and could not place his among them.

I wanted the notebook to explain him. Perhaps he had arrived late and stood at the back. Perhaps he had sat outside and changed his mind. Perhaps the trip had nothing to do with me. Northbridge had hospitals, warehouses, old friends, and thousands of reasons to buy fuel. The notebook offered distance, date, and price. It would not supply intention.

Imani offered seven hundred dollars for the car. I accepted six hundred because she would need to replace the battery. We completed the paperwork at my father’s kitchen table. I gave her both keys, the wool blanket, the jumper cables, and the jars of bolts. I kept the notebook. This made no practical sense; its information belonged with the car. I told Imani I needed it for the estate records, which was not true.

She drove away just after noon. The Vela hesitated before turning onto the road, then the pale door flashed once between the trees. I stood beneath the carport holding a history that documented almost everything except what I wanted to know.

Months later, I still open the notebook to September 18. I used to think my father’s flaw was that he could measure fuel but not feeling. That judgment now seems too convenient. I am the one trying to turn 814 kilometers into a sentence he never said. His columns remain narrow and exact. Outside them is the part of the journey neither of us recorded.
cases.outputConstraint: 1000-1200 words; include one title and no subheadings; preserve the unresolved ambiguity; output only the finished essay.

cases.title: Cedar Bay's Late Shift Link Pilot
cases.prompt:
Rewrite the source below as an engaging public-facing policy explainer for informed non-specialists. Preserve every number, date, population, definition, condition, comparison, result, limitation, uncertainty, and distinction between evidence and estimate. Do not add outside information or imply causation where the source does not. Write 1200-1450 words. Include a strong title and 3-5 descriptive subheadings. Use prose rather than a table. Output only the finished explainer.

SOURCE

Cedar Bay’s Late Shift Link was a six-month transportation pilot for workers whose shifts ended after most scheduled bus service. It operated from April 1 through September 30, 2025. The city commissioned the pilot after a 2024 travel survey found that 18 percent of workers in the Harbor and East Junction employment districts regularly finished work after 11 p.m. The survey covered 2,406 workers at thirty-one sites. It did not include independent contractors or people working at businesses with fewer than ten employees.

Under the pilot, eligible workers could request a shared van between 10:30 p.m. and 2:30 a.m. Trips had to begin at one of twelve participating workplaces and end at a home address or transit stop within the city boundary. Workers booked through an app or a telephone dispatcher. A trip cost the rider two dollars. The city paid the remaining operating cost.

Twelve employers participated: four hotels, three food-processing plants, two hospitals, two warehouses, and one casino. Together, they identified 1,608 eligible workers. Enrollment required a worker ID and one proof of home address, but the city later allowed employers to verify addresses for workers without a document in their own name. By the end of the pilot, 1,124 workers had enrolled and 738 had taken at least one completed ride. Among riders, 214 took ten or more rides, and seventy-six took at least forty.

The system received 9,840 bookings. Operators completed 8,906 rides, riders canceled 611 before dispatch, and 323 were recorded as no-shows. The median pickup wait was eighteen minutes. The ninetieth-percentile wait was forty-one minutes. During eleven nights with major events or severe rain, the median wait rose to thirty-three minutes. The service standard called for pickups within thirty minutes, but the operator met that standard for 76 percent of completed rides.

The vans delivered 72 percent of riders directly to a home address and 28 percent to one of five overnight bus stops. Shared trips carried an average of 1.7 passengers. The operator refused forty-seven requests because the destination was outside the city boundary and nineteen because no accessible vehicle was available at the requested time. Three of the nineteen riders accepted a later accessible pickup. The report did not document how the other sixteen traveled.

The pilot budget was $1.2 million. Actual public spending was $814,000, including dispatch, vehicle operations, insurance, evaluation, and $62,000 in one-time software setup. Fares contributed $17,812 and were returned to the program budget. Using total public spending, the cost was about $91 per completed ride. Excluding one-time software setup, the cost was about $84 per completed ride. These figures do not include administrative time contributed by participating employers.

For comparison, the transit agency estimated that extending two fixed bus routes until 2:30 a.m. for the same six months would have cost between $690,000 and $940,000. That estimate assumed hourly service and did not include stops at four of the twelve workplaces. Because the city did not operate the extended routes, the estimate cannot show how many people would have used them or what each passenger trip would have cost.

The evaluation examined work attendance at six participating employers that supplied consistent payroll data. These employers accounted for 903 eligible workers. Evaluators matched each one with a nonparticipating employer in the same industry and district. In the three months before the pilot, employees at participating sites missed 4.7 percent of scheduled late shifts, compared with 4.5 percent at matched sites. During the pilot, the rates were 2.9 percent and 3.9 percent, respectively. After adjusting for employer size and month, the estimated difference in the change was 1.2 percentage points, with a 95 percent confidence interval from 0.3 to 2.1 percentage points.

The attendance result does not establish that the van service caused the improvement. Employers were not randomly assigned. Two participating employers changed supervisors during the pilot, one introduced an attendance bonus, and matched employers may have differed in ways the analysis did not measure. Payroll data also showed whether a shift was missed, not why. The evaluators described the attendance finding as promising but not conclusive.

A rider survey was sent to all 738 people who completed a trip, and 486 responded. Seventy-eight percent of respondents said the service made it easier to accept late shifts. Fifty-four percent said they had previously paid for a taxi or app-based ride at least twice a month. Thirty-one percent said they had previously walked more than thirty minutes after a late shift. Fourteen percent reported missing at least one shift in the prior three months because they lacked transportation. Because the survey was conducted only after the pilot and depended on memory, these responses cannot measure how behavior changed. Riders with strong positive or negative views may also have been more likely to answer.

Demographic information was optional. Among the 591 riders who provided it, 64 percent identified as women, 33 percent as men, and 3 percent as nonbinary or another gender. Forty-two percent reported household income below $35,000. Twenty-seven percent reported a disability, but the survey did not distinguish disabilities that affected transportation. Demographic information was not available for all eligible workers, so the report could not determine whether any group enrolled at a higher or lower rate than its share of the eligible population.

There were three reported safety incidents. In one, a van was involved in a low-speed collision with no injuries. In another, a rider reported threatening language from a second rider; the operator separated their future bookings. In the third, a driver ended a trip because a rider would not wear a seat belt. No serious injuries were reported. The small number of trips and reliance on reported incidents mean the pilot cannot establish that shared vans are safer or less safe than other late-night options.

The pilot did not measure greenhouse-gas emissions. Vans traveled 61,440 vehicle kilometers, including 19,870 kilometers without passengers. Evaluators noted that combining riders could avoid some individual car trips, while empty travel and trips that otherwise would have used a bus could add vehicle travel. They concluded that the available data were insufficient to estimate the net effect.

Operational problems were concentrated at predictable times. Friday demand between 11:00 p.m. and 12:15 a.m. exceeded capacity on fourteen of the twenty-six Fridays. Hospital shift changes created a second peak at 1:00 a.m. Telephone bookings took a median of six minutes, compared with ninety seconds in the app. Dispatchers said address verification was the main source of delay on phone calls. Riders who booked by phone were older on average than app users, but age information was missing for 38 percent of riders.

The evaluation presented three policy options. The first was to end the pilot and rely on existing overnight routes. The second was to extend the shared-van model for twelve months with a budget cap of $1.5 million. The third was to fund later fixed-route buses and use vans only for workplaces more than 800 meters from a late-night stop. The report did not recommend a permanent program. It recommended a twelve-month extension with three changes: require employers to contribute 20 percent of trip costs, reserve two accessible vehicles throughout operating hours, and test fixed departure windows on Fridays.

The city council voted 6–3 in November 2025 to fund the recommended extension. The extension began January 5, 2026. Its results are not part of the six-month evaluation.
cases.outputConstraint: 1200-1450 words; include one title and 3-5 descriptive subheadings; prose rather than a table; output only the finished explainer.
